title: The City of Kitchener is here to help you get registration ready
content: We’re here to help you get registration ready.  Whether  you're signing up for seasonal programs or camps, drop by your local centre, aquatic  centre or Kitchener  City Hall for personalized help ahead of registration day.

Staff can help you browse available programs, answer  questions and confirm registration dates.  Get help creating or updating your  ActiveNet account. We’re here to help you through account setup, password recovery and getting ready for registration day.
Build your Wishlist to stay ahead. Pick your top program choices in advance.
Learn how to register online with confidence. Staff will guide you through the  ActiveNet platform, including how to search, filter, and check out quickly on registration day.
With preparation, your registration will be faster,  easier and less stressful.
Ask as many questions as you need. No question is too  small, our team is here to make sure you feel confident and informed.
Upcoming registration dates:
Spring registration
Tuesday, March 10 at 8:30 a.m. for Kitchener residents
Tuesday, March 17 at 8:30 a.m. for non-residents.
Summer camp registration
Tuesday, March 24 at 8:30 a.m. for Kitchener residents
Tuesday, March 31 at 8:30 a.m. for non-residents.
Visit your local  centre, aquatic  centre or Kitchener  City Hall for in-person support.
Find out more about upcoming programs and camps on  our website .

title: Get ready to apply for a 2026 LoveMyHood Matching Grant
content: Do you have  an idea for a great  neighbourhood project ?  The  LoveMyHood Matching Grant supports projects that bring neighbours together, build connections, and create new programs and spaces that make Kitchener a great community. Past projects include little libraries,  community gardens, greening projects, neighbourhood pop-up markets, street parties, art murals, neighbourhood programs and more.

The City of Kitchener’s  LoveMyHood Matching Grant provides one-time funding for resident-led projects and neighbourhood initiatives of up to $30,000 , depending on location and scope of the project. The first grant intake deadline for this year is Thursday, March 5, 2026.

Before you submit your application,  connect with the team from the  Neighbourhood Development Office  for assistance. Our project coordinators will guide you through the grant process, share essential information & best practices, help you navigate obstacles, build a budget,  and advocate for your project every step of the way, helping you to take your project idea from dream to reality!

The  LoveMyHood Matching Grant funding is awarded three times per year,  and  the Grant Selection Committee will review applications after each intake. Submissions for each review period are due on the following dates:
Review Period 1 - Thursday, March 5, 2026
Review Period  2 - Thursday, June 11, 2026
Review Period 3 - Thursday, October 29, 2026
To apply online, or for more information about the  LoveMyHood Matching Grant, visit LoveMyHood.ca/LMHgrant.

title: City of Kitchener receives 2026 Municipality of the Year and Top 100 Events in Ontario awards from Festival & Events Ontario
content: Kitchener, ON -  The City of Kitchener is  the  proud  recipient of the  2026 Municipality of the Year award from  Festivals & Events Ontario (FEO), along with two Top 100 Events in Ontario awards for  KidsPark and Christkindl Market .  The awards recognize municipalities, towns, or cities who have worked to provide an atmosphere beneficial to successful festivals and events.

Presented at FEO’s annual Awards Gala and Dinner on Wednesday, February 25,  2026 th ese  honours  recognize the City’s growing slate of signature festivals, commitment to community  partnerships and its ability to create memorable shared experiences.

“Every year, our special events calendar  brings  joy and puts smiles on the faces of  more than  350,000 residents and visitors to Downtown  Kitchener,”  said  Mayor Berry Vrbanovic. “This  success is shaped by our history of strong partnerships with local community organizations and cultural groups ,  the passion and drive to support  great events from both our city council and Kitchener event s teams, and of course,  our commitment to  “Support Local” with great  Canadian  talent.  We’re grateful to Festival & Events Ontario for recognizing this work with the se awards.”
The City of Kitchener hosts some of the region’s most  beloved traditions and premiere events , at no cost to attendees, including:
Christkindl Market
New Year’s Night of Legends
KidsPark
Cruising on King
Wayback Festival
Canada Day
Endless Summer

The  City also partners with the area’s new and signature third-party event organizers . Oktoberfest, the Kitchener Blues Festival, Kitchener Ribfest and Craft Beer Show, the Multicultural Festival and Caribana Ignite are led by community organizations,  with  on-the-ground  support from City staff who  oversee  logistics  and provide  planning support.  This partnership model allows  local  festivals to grow  and deliver exceptional  experiences for residents, while  leveraging  the  expertise of City staff . Oktoberfest, the  Multicultural Festival and the Kitchener Ribfest and Craft Beer Show were all recognized as Top 100 Events in Ontario at the awards ceremony .

"There’s no better feeling than seeing  thousands of  our friends and  neighbours  creating shared memories together ,” said  Steve Roth, Manager of Special Events. “Our small but mighty team is  proud to  bring our community together through these events.  We’re e xcited  for  what lies ahead for 2026.”
Learn more about our  upcoming event season at  KitchenerEvents.ca

title: Second annual Downtown Showdown brings the Kitchener Rangers to Carl Zehr Square this March Break
content: Kitchener,  ON  – Hockey lovers, mark your calendars: the second annual Downtown Showdown is returning to Kitchener’s Carl Zehr Square this March Break with even more winter fun planned for the whole  family. The City of Kitchener and  the  Kitchener Rangers are partnering to bring the Kitchener Rangers experience to Downtown Kitchener on Wednesday, March 18 from noon to 5 p.m.

“Downtown Showdown became a family  favourite after last year’s  inaugural  event and we immediately knew we had to bring it back again this March Break,” said Mayor Berry Vrbanovic. “We’re thrilled to give our whole community the opportunity to see the skill of our  beloved  home team and come together for a winter celebration for all ages.  We’re grateful to have wonderful partners in the Kitchener Rangers who are as committed as we are to creating a day of fun  focused  around Canada’s beloved sport of hockey that everyone can enjoy.”

The Kitchener Rangers will play a live 3-on-3  road hockey  tournament with six short games culminating in a final championship game. Players will be  signing autographs all day  long and Tex will be roaming the crowd for photos.

All day long, enjoy live music from  a DJ and high-energy performer  The Trevor Show , free games and activities, hot chocolate, maple taffy, and see fire trucks, police cars and the City’s electric Zamboni up close. Weather  permitting, the Carl Zehr Square ice rink will be open for a community skate.  Kitchener Minor Hockey  will also be on-site grilling hot dogs available for a donation to the organization.

“Last year  was such a resounding success and the way the community came together for this event was something we knew we had to make an annual tradition. Everyone in our organization from the business staff to the players could not be more excited to be a part of this day,” said Kitchener Rangers Chief Operating Officer, Joe Birch. “Being a community owned team means a lot to us, so we are always looking for ways for our players to interact with fans around the city.”
Whether  you're a hockey fan or simply ready for a fun day out this March Break, there is something for you at Downtown Showdown! Learn more at Kitchener.ca/DowntownShowdown

title: Help guide the future of Kitchener’s nature education and stewardship programs
content: The City of Kitchener  is looking to expand its Kitchener Natural Areas Program (KNAP) , an environmental initiative that promotes community involvement in Kitchener’s natural spaces and urban  forest. The program currently offers  nature-based wellness experiences and learning opportunities within the community.  The  City is exploring how  to expand KNAP’s volunteer and educational programs to give Kitchener residents more opportunities to learn about and engage in the protection and enhancement of Kitchener’s trees and natural areas.
We are  seeking community feedback until March 15.  Responses  will be used to help shape  new programs, including learning opportunities and hands-on volunteer experiences.
Follow the project’s progress  and share your ideas  at  EngageWR.ca/KNAP and learn more about the KNAP program at Kitchener.ca/KNAP .

title: City of Kitchener declares snow event with parking ban starting at 2 a.m. February 25, 2026
content: Kitchener, ON  - The City of Kitchener has declared a snow event based on weather forecast information that includes the potential for over  10 cm of snow in the next 24 hours . Residents have until  2  a.m.  on Wednesday,  February 25,  2026 to remove their parked cars from city streets.  This helps ensure our plows and snow clearing equipment  are able to effectively and efficiently clear snow from roadways across the city .
The parking ban will remain in effect for 24 hours. Overnight parking exemptions are cancelled to allow for snow clearing.

During a snow event, the City's tag-and-tow process takes effect. Residents are prohibited from parking their cars on city streets at any time when a snow event has been declared by the  City. The ticket for parking on the street during a snow event is $100. Vehicles can also be towed if a snow operator is unable to get down a city street and contacts enforcement staff on patrol.

During a snow event, the  City activates its Getting Around online map , which shows the roads that have recently been serviced. This map is updated regularly beginning at the time the snow event goes into effect and for 24 hours after it has ended.

Subscribe to the City’s  Snow Events news feed to be notified when a snow event has been declared, cancelled or extended. You can also follow the City of Kitchener’s Instagram ,  Facebook and X (formerly Twitter) accounts for updates.
For more information about snow  events visit Kitchener.ca/snow or call our Corporate Contact Centre at 519-741-2345.
For more information :
Krystin Scheels  Communications and Marketing Associate  City of Kitchener  media@kitchener.ca

title: Calling all revellers: Caribana Ignite returns to Downtown Kitchener for 2026
content: The rhythm,  colour and electric energy of Carnival return to downtown Kitchener this summer as the third annual Caribana Ignite Festival lights up the city on August 21 and 22, 2026. Already one of the province’s fastest-growing Caribbean experiences, Caribana Ignite is calling on revellers (from frontline to backline and everything in between) across Ontario to help make this year’s festival the biggest and most vibrant celebration yet.

This is an open invitation to  mas bands, section leaders, new or seasoned revellers, Big Mas  creators and  performers, or anyone with a costume from any carnival –  anywhere in the world to bring their energy, their artistry and their vibes to the road in Kitchener. Whether you’re an established ensemble playing “ ole mas ” or an individual ready to jump back into the Carnival spirit,  Caribana Ignite wants you on the road. This is the ultimate RE-WINE!

All revelers  participating in the Caribana Ignite Kitchener parade must register online at CaribanaIgnite.com by the April 30 ,  2026 deadline. Round-trip transportation (based on availability) will be provided from Toronto, London, and Hamilton, making it easy for participants across the province to join the festivities.
Friday, August 21 – Glow Party
The festival kicks off with an electrifying family Glow Party in downtown Kitchener — music, lights,  dance and nonstop vibes as the downtown core transforms into an electric pre-Carnival party.
Saturday, August 22 – Parade & Street Party

The Carnival Parade brings thousands into the streets with vibrant costumes, pulsating  soca and the unmatched spirit of Caribbean culture. The celebration continues at the massive street party in front of Kitchener City Hall, keeping the energy high into the evening.

“Caribana Ignite has become one of the most  exciting  events in our community’s  summer calendar,” shared Mayor Berry Vrbanovic. “We’re thrilled to welcome performers, families and revelers from across Ontario to share the joy,  creativity and vibrant celebration of Caribbean culture  that  this event  brings to our streets. Festivals like  Caribana Ignite  strengthen our community  and remind us of the power of celebration to bring people together.”

Geraldine Stafford, Festival Director, Caribana Ignite Kitchener added “ Overwhelmingly, what we continue to hear is that Caribana Ignite Kitchener brings people back to the good old days of Caribana. That spirit, that nostalgia, is exactly what we set out to recreate — and it is why the concept of ‘re‑wine’ resonates so strongly.  Whether  you’ve played mas in Trinidad, St. Lucia, Grenada, Miami, or anywhere the Caribbean diaspora celebrates, we are calling all revelers to join us on the road.  We are deeply grateful to the City of Kitchener for its continued support in welcoming Carnival in such a bold and vibrant way to the heart of the downtown core.”

Caribana Ignite continues to grow as a signature cultural event, celebrating Caribbean heritage while bringing communities together from across Ontario. With more dancers, more music, and more mas, 2026 will be the most unforgettable year yet.

title: Kitchener Fire Department: Major incident report – February 22, 2026
content: Earlier today, Kitchener Fire (KFD) responded to a fire at an automotive and tire shop located on McIntyre Drive in Kitchener. Multiple fire crews were dispatched and arrived on scene to find a fire within the commercial structure. Crews worked quickly to bring the fire under control.
There are no reported injuries to occupants or firefighters.
Primary searches have been completed by KFD crews. An investigation into the cause of the fire will begin once the building is deemed safe to enter.
The extent of the damage to the structure and its contents is not yet known. Damage estimates are not currently available.
For more information: Corporate Communications City of Kitchener media@kitchener.ca

title: Kitchener Fire Department: Major incident report – February 22, 2026
content: Earlier this morning, Kitchener Fire (KFD) responded to a call for a residential structure fire at Elmsdale Drive. The fire originated in a bedroom on the upper floor of the home. Multiple fire crews were dispatched and arrived quickly on scene to bring the situation under control.
Three residents were transported to hospital for assessment. All have since been treated and released.
The home sustained fire and smoke damage on the upper floor. The fire is not being treated as suspicious. Damage estimates are not available at this time.
For more information: Corporate Communications City of Kitchener media@kitchener.ca

title: Help Shape the Future of Tree Conservation in Kitchener
content: Trees are a vital part of Kitchener’s urban landscape. They clean our air, provide shade, support mental  well-being and offer habitat for local wildlife. While the City of Kitchener actively maintains over 72,000 trees on public land, trees on private property also play a crucial role in growing and sustaining our urban tree canopy.

To better understand how residents care for and manage trees on their own properties, the City is inviting community members to  participate in a new survey focused on Private Tree Conservation and Management.

This survey is part of a broader effort to protect and expand Kitchener’s tree canopy, which currently covers 27 per cent of the city. With goals of reaching 30 per cent coverage in every ward by 2050 and 33 per cent citywide by 2070, private trees are key to achieving long-term success.

The survey asks residents to share their experiences with tree planting, maintenance, and removal on private property. It also seeks feedback on  residents’ knowledge and experience with  the City’s  current  Private Tree Bylaw, which requires a permit for the removal of trees on lots  one acre in size or greater . By gathering input from homeowners, renters, and property managers, the City hopes to improve  our bylaw and  policies, support responsible tree care and ensure that private trees continue to thrive.

Whether  you’ve planted a tree in your yard, had to remove one or simply enjoy the shade they provide, your voice matters. The survey takes just a few minutes to complete and will help shape future policies,  programs,  practices and bylaws that affect tree conservation across Kitchener.
The survey can be found on the project’s Engage Kitchener page  https://www.engagewr.ca/privatetrees .
